abstract: Reconstructing dynamic scenes with complex human-object interactions is a fundamental challenge in computer vision and graphics. Existing Gaussian Splatting methods either rely on human pose priors while neglecting dynamic objects, or approximate all motions within a single field, limiting their ability to capture interaction-rich dynamics. To address this gap, we propose Human-Object Interaction Gaussian Splatting (HOIGS), which explicitly models interaction-induced deformation between humans and objects through a cross-attention-based HOI module. Distinct deformation baselines are employed to extract features: HexPlane for humans and Cubic Hermite Spline (CHS) for objects. By integrating these heterogeneous features, HOIGS effectively captures interdependent motions and improves deformation estimation in scenarios involving occlusion, contact, and object manipulation. Comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art human-centric and 4D Gaussian approaches, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling human-object interactions for high-fidelity reconstruction.

citations.fullCitation: Kerbl, B., Kopanas, G., Leimkühler, T., Drettakis, G.: 3d gaussian splatting for real-time radiance field rendering. ACM Trans. Graph. 42(4), 139–1 (2023)
citations.justification: This is the seminal paper that introduced 3D Gaussian Splatting, the core real-time rendering and scene representation technique upon which the main paper, HOIGS, is fundamentally built. HOIGS extends this foundational framework to handle the complexities of dynamic human-object interactions.
citations.title: 3d gaussian splatting for real-time radiance field rendering

citations.fullCitation: Wu, G., Yi, T., Fang, J., Xie, L., Zhang, X., Wei, W., Liu, W., Tian, Q., Wang, X.: 4d gaussian splatting for real-time dynamic scene rendering. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. pp. 20310–20320 (2024)
citations.justification: This paper introduces the extension of Gaussian Splatting to dynamic scenes (4DGS), representing a primary category of methods that HOIGS is directly compared against. The main paper argues that such general dynamic scene methods fail to capture the specific details of human articulation and interaction, a limitation HOIGS aims to overcome.
citations.title: 4d gaussian splatting for real-time dynamic scene rendering

citations.fullCitation: Moon, G., Shiratori, T., Saito, S.: Expressive whole-body 3d gaussian avatar. In: European Conference on Computer Vision. pp. 19–35. Springer (2024)
citations.justification: This paper, referred to as ExAvatar, is a state-of-the-art human-centric reconstruction method that HOIGS frequently uses as a key baseline for comparison. It is cited to highlight the limitations of prior art that focuses solely on the human actor while failing to render interacting dynamic objects, which is the central problem HOIGS addresses.
citations.title: Expressive whole-body 3d gaussian avatar

citations.fullCitation: Liu, J.W., Cao, Y.P., Yang, T., Xu, Z., Keppo, J., Shan, Y., Qie, X., Shou, M.Z.: Hosnerf: Dynamic human-object-scene neural radiance fields from a single video. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision. pp. 18483–18494 (2023)
citations.justification: This work addresses the exact same problem of dynamic human-object-scene reconstruction, but with a NeRF-based approach. Critically, it also introduces the HOSNeRF dataset, which is a primary benchmark used for quantitative and qualitative evaluation in the main paper, making this citation essential for understanding the experimental context.
citations.title: Hosnerf: Dynamic human-object-scene neural radiance fields from a single video

citations.fullCitation: Cao, A., Johnson, J.: Hexplane: A fast representation for dynamic scenes. In: Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. pp. 130–141 (2023)
citations.justification: This paper introduces the HexPlane representation, which is a core technical component adopted by HOIGS for modeling human deformation. The main paper explicitly uses a HexPlane-based deformation field to capture fine-grained human motion and spatiotemporal features, making this citation key to understanding the proposed methodology.
citations.title: Hexplane: A fast representation for dynamic scenes

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "HOIGS: Human-Object Interaction Gaussian Splatting from Monocular Videos."

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by:
*   **Taewoo Kim, Suwoong Yeom, Jaehyun Pyun, Joonsik Nam, Yun-Seong Jeong, and Suk-Ju Kang** from the Department of Electronics Engineering, Sogang University, South Korea.
*   **Geonho Cha and Dongyoon Wee** from NAVER Cloud Corp, South Korea.
*   **Kyeongbo Kong** from the Department of Electrical & Electronics Engineering, Pusan National University, South Korea.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The reconstruction of dynamic scenes involving human-object interactions (HOI) from monocular video is a fundamental challenge in computer vision and graphics. This capability is critical for applications such as immersive virtual reality, metaverse content creation, and 3D animation. However, existing methods encounter limitations when attempting to accurately model the complex spatial and temporal dependencies inherent in HOI scenarios.

Previous research efforts generally fall into two categories:
*   **Human-centric scene reconstruction methods:** These approaches often combine parametric human models (e.g., SMPL) with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). While effective at capturing articulated human motion and soft-tissue deformation by leveraging strong pose priors and techniques like Linear Blend Skinning (LBS), they typically neglect dynamic objects. Objects are frequently treated as static background elements or omitted entirely, leading to artifacts such as ghosting, floating geometry, or incomplete scene representations in interaction-rich dynamics. These frameworks often fail to model the mutual influence between humans and objects as distinct dynamic entities.
*   **General dynamic scene reconstruction methods (4D Gaussian Splatting):** These methods aim to represent all moving elements within a scene without semantic distinction. They commonly employ implicit deformation fields or parameterize motion trajectories. However, by treating humans and objects uniformly, these methods often struggle to capture the high-frequency details of human articulation and the specific rigid dynamics of manipulated objects. Implicit deformation fields can over-smooth complex motions, making it difficult to stably represent non-linear trajectories and hindering the accurate depiction of close contact, occlusion, and manipulation where human and object motions are tightly coupled.

HOIGS addresses a gap between these two lines of research. It proposes a unified framework that overcomes the limitations of existing methods by explicitly modeling the mutual dependencies of human and object motions. Rather than isolating humans or treating the entire scene as a singular motion field, HOIGS disentangles the representation of humans and objects, applies specialized deformation strategies appropriate for each entity, and then couples them through a novel interaction mechanism.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to achieve high-quality view synthesis and 4D reconstruction of dynamic scenes characterized by complex human-object interactions using only monocular video input. This overarching goal is motivated by several specific challenges observed in current approaches to dynamic scene reconstruction.

The key motivations and objectives include:

*   **Addressing limitations of human-centric models:** Existing human-centric methods, while proficient at reconstructing human bodies, often fail to account for dynamic objects with which humans interact. This leads to unrealistic reconstructions where objects appear static, ghosted, or are completely absent. A core objective is to integrate dynamic object reconstruction alongside human reconstruction without compromising human fidelity.
*   **Overcoming deficiencies in general 4D dynamic scene models:** General 4D Gaussian Splatting methods, designed for broad dynamic scenes, frequently struggle with the specific complexities of human-object interactions. They tend to over-smooth fine-grained articulated motions of humans and rigid movements of objects, particularly when interactions involve close contact, occlusions, or precise manipulations. The motivation is to capture these high-frequency details and physically plausible motions more accurately.
*   **Explicitly modeling human-object dependencies:** The core motivation is the recognition that human and object motions during interaction are interdependent. Current methods often treat these entities in isolation or as part of a monolithic dynamic field, failing to capture the bidirectional influence that defines realistic interactions. This work aims to introduce a mechanism to explicitly model this mutual dependency.
*   **Developing entity-specific deformation strategies:** Humans and objects possess fundamentally distinct physical and kinematic properties. The human body exhibits complex articulated motion and local soft-tissue deformations, while manipulated objects are often predominantly rigid and follow continuous trajectories. A key objective is to leverage these distinctions by employing specialized deformation models tailored to each entity for more accurate and plausible motion capture.
*   **Mitigating interaction artifacts:** In scenarios involving close contact, heavy occlusion, and object manipulation, existing methods frequently produce artifacts, especially around interaction zones (e.g., hands manipulating objects). The motivation is to reduce these artifacts by introducing an interaction-aware mechanism that refines the deformations of both humans and objects based on their mutual influence.
*   **Enabling downstream applications:** By achieving high-fidelity HOI reconstruction, the research aims to support the creation of more realistic content for applications such as immersive virtual reality, the metaverse, and 3D animation, where accurate depiction of human-object interaction is essential.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

HOIGS operates as a unified framework that reconstructs dynamic human-object interaction scenes by first disentangling human and object motions, and then unifying them through interaction-aware refinements. The methodology comprises entity-specific deformation baselines, a cross-attention-based interaction module, and an integrated optimization strategy.

**4.1 Entity-Specific Deformation Baselines**

To account for the distinct physical and kinematic properties of humans and objects, HOIGS employs separate deformation models:

*   **Object Deformation (Cubic Hermite Spline - CHS):**
    *   **Object Initialization:** The process begins by segmenting the target object region from a representative frame. A canonical 3D Gaussian point cloud for the object is then generated using a diffusion prior guided by Score Distillation Sampling (SDS). To address scale ambiguity inherent in diffusion-based priors, a global scale factor is estimated by comparing the 2D mask bounding box area with the projected 3D Gaussian bounding box area across frames. Canonical Gaussian means are scaled and translated into camera space using masked depth, then transformed into world coordinates using camera rotation and translation from COLMAP.
    *   **Spline-based Motion Modeling:** The centroids of the object Gaussians are modeled using a Cubic Hermite Spline (CHS). This approach ensures continuity in both position and velocity, aiming to prevent abrupt jumps in the object's trajectory. Learnable control points, representing mean positions at keyframes, and explicit velocity vectors (tangent vectors) are optimized. This reinterpretation of tangent vectors as learnable velocity allows for capturing complex dynamic behaviors. Object rotation is modeled via Spherical Linear Interpolation (SLERP) of keyframe rotations, opacity is time-dependent, and scale remains constant to enforce object rigidity.
*   **Human Deformation (HexPlane-based with SMPL-X Priors):**
    *   A HexPlane-based representation is adopted for human deformation, leveraging its efficiency in encoding spatiotemporal features. Time-invariant spatial features from the HexPlane encode the texture of a canonical T-pose mesh. An MLP head processes these features to predict Gaussian attributes (color, opacity, position offset, rotation, scale, skinning weights) in the canonical space.
    *   The canonical representation is deformed into the posed world space using Linear Blend Skinning (LBS), driven by SMPL-X pose parameters and a learnable scale factor. To ensure geometric fidelity, a depth supervision loss is applied, comparing rendered depth with estimates from an off-the-shelf depth estimator.

**4.2 Cross-Attention Interaction Module (HOI Module)**

This module explicitly models the mutual influence between humans and objects using cross-attention:

*   **Feature Extraction:**
    *   **Human Features (`F_Human`):** Time-varying features are extracted directly from the HexPlane. The human body is semantically divided into 16 parts (e.g., head, torso, hands) based on the SMPL-X model. For each part, HexPlane features sampled at its vertices are averaged to form a part-specific feature token. This yields a set of 16 tokens, each a 96-dimensional vector.
    *   **Object Features (`F_Object`):** Features are constructed from each keyframe's 3-dimensional velocity vector and a 29-dimensional learnable embedding. These are concatenated, interpolated by CHS to provide continuous motion features, and then combined with the temporal variable and projected through a shallow MLP. This results in N_obj tokens, each a 32-dimensional vector.
*   **Interaction Mechanism:** Cross-attention is performed in both directions (human-to-object and object-to-human) to capture bidirectional dependencies. Queries, keys, and values are linearly projected from the extracted features. A distance mask is incorporated into the attention computation to filter out non-interacting background objects. This mask is based on the Euclidean distance between the object's Gaussian center and the human's pelvis position; objects beyond a certain threshold (e.g., human arm length) are masked out.
*   **Residual Corrections:** The interaction-aware features resulting from cross-attention are used to parameterize residual corrections. These include SMPL-X pose refinements (for body, left hand, and right hand) derived from `F'_Human` and object Gaussian mean offsets derived from `F'_Object`. These residuals are then added to the human (HexPlane+LBS) and object (CHS) deformation outputs to produce interaction-consistent trajectories.

**4.3 Optimization**

An integrated optimization strategy jointly refines the human deformation field, object motion splines, and static background representation. The total objective function (`L`) combines several loss terms:

*   **Human Loss (`L_human`):** Includes photometric losses (L1, D-SSIM, LPIPS) on the cropped human region, a mesh-based facial consistency term, and a Laplacian regularizer for body shape stability.
*   **Object Loss (`L_object`):** L1 and D-SSIM photometric errors are computed exclusively on the segmented object regions.
*   **Scene Loss (`L_scene`):** L1 and D-SSIM photometric errors are computed on the static background regions, excluding human and object masks.
*   **Depth Loss (`L_depth`):** An L1 distance between the rendered depth and the depth estimated by an off-the-shelf depth estimator.

The weighting of these losses is empirically set to balance their contributions during optimization.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments conducted on multiple datasets demonstrated that HOIGS consistently achieved high performance in reconstructing dynamic human-object interaction scenes.

**5.1 Quantitative Evaluation**

*   **Superiority across Datasets:** HOIGS consistently achieved the highest Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) and lowest Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS) across the HOSNeRF, BEHAVE, and ARCTIC datasets. This indicates improved visual quality and perceptual realism compared to baseline methods.
    *   On the **HOSNeRF** dataset, HOIGS outperformed 10 baseline methods, including various NeRF-based and 4D Gaussian Splatting approaches, as well as human-centric models. For instance, HOIGS achieved an average PSNR of 25.89 and LPIPS of 0.114, surpassing ExAvatar (PSNR 24.35, LPIPS 0.147) and 4DGS (PSNR 24.96, LPIPS 0.169).
    *   On the **BEHAVE** dataset, HOIGS maintained its lead, achieving an average PSNR of 31.51 and LPIPS of 0.034, which was notably higher than ExAvatar (PSNR 28.47, LPIPS 0.041).
    *   On the **ARCTIC** dataset, HOIGS outperformed HOLD [8], a specialized hand-object interaction baseline, despite HOLD focusing only on hand reconstruction. HOIGS also surpassed general 4DGS methods in full-body interaction sequences.
*   **Geometric Accuracy (Supplementary Material):** An evaluation on the ARCTIC dataset using Chamfer Distance (CD) for geometric proximity between reconstructed hand and object point clouds showed that HOIGS achieved significantly lower distances (0.1337 m$^2$) compared to HOLD (1.5035 m$^2$). This suggests superior geometric accuracy and more physically plausible hand-object contact.

**5.2 Qualitative Evaluation**

*   **Photorealistic Novel Views:** HOIGS produced photorealistic novel views without the severe artifacts (e.g., ghosting, floating geometry, body-background overlap) observed in baseline approaches.
*   **Mitigation of Artifacts:** Dynamic-scene models like D3DGS and Ex4DGS exhibited ghosting artifacts due to their inability to disentangle human and object motions. Human-centric models such as ExAvatar failed to render dynamic objects or showed misalignment issues. In contrast, HOIGS accurately represented complex human-object interactions without such visual degradations.
*   **Fidelity in Interaction Regions:** Close-up views of interaction regions demonstrated that HOIGS maintained high visual fidelity and sharp reconstructions, closely matching ground truth even under severe occlusion and low-resolution conditions.

**5.3 Ablation Studies**

Ablation studies validated the contributions of the proposed components:

*   **Object Deformation:** Replacing the CHS-based object deformation with a simple MLP resulted in a performance drop, confirming the effectiveness of CHS. The CHS-based baseline deformation improved PSNR by approximately 0.5.
*   **HOI Module:** Removing the explicit Cross-Attention HOI module, leading to reliance solely on velocity for interaction modeling, resulted in an approximate 0.65 drop in PSNR. This highlights the necessity of explicitly modeling human-object interactions.
*   **Human Feature Design:** Using time-varying HexPlane features for human representation, instead of simpler parameter embeddings, yielded a 0.2 increase in PSNR, indicating the advantage of this feature design.
*   **Object Priors (Supplementary Material):** The Diffusion Prior used for object initialization proved more effective than alternative priors like MASt3R or monocular metric depth estimation, achieving higher average PSNR. This was attributed to its ability to plausibly complete 3D geometry in occluded regions, providing a more robust starting point for deformation.

**5.4 Computational Performance (Supplementary Material)**

*   **Inference Speed:** HOIGS achieved an inference speed of 44.27 Frames Per Second (FPS) on the HOSNeRF dataset, which is comparable to other real-time 4DGS methods (e.g., Ex4DGS at 46.38 FPS, D3DGS at 37.79 FPS) and suitable for real-time applications.
*   **Training Time:** The training time for HOIGS (approximately 5 hours per scene) was longer than some baselines, reflecting the complexity of explicitly modeling mutual dependencies and gradient backpropagation through the attention mechanism. However, this is an offline cost.

**5.5 Robustness to External Modules (Supplementary Material)**

*   **Consistency across Priors:** HOIGS demonstrated robust performance with consistent PSNR (ranging from 25.44 to 26.1) across various combinations of external segmentation and depth estimation priors (e.g., Samurai, SAMv2, MaskRCNN for segmentation; MetricV2, Video Depth Anything, DepthCrafter for depth). This indicates that the method is not overly sensitive to variations in preprocessing quality from off-the-shelf tools.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The development of HOIGS represents an advancement in the field of dynamic scene reconstruction, particularly for scenarios involving complex human-object interactions. Its significance stems from several key aspects:

*   **Bridging a Critical Gap:** HOIGS effectively bridges the gap between human-centric and general dynamic scene reconstruction methods. By explicitly disentangling and then coupling human and object representations, it overcomes the limitations of prior approaches that either neglect dynamic objects or over-smooth intricate interaction dynamics.
*   **Enhanced Realism and Fidelity:** The method's ability to achieve consistently higher quantitative metrics (PSNR, LPIPS) and produce qualitatively superior, artifact-free reconstructions demonstrates a notable improvement in visual realism and fidelity for interaction-rich scenes. The explicit modeling of mutual dependencies via the HOI module contributes to physically plausible contact and motion, especially in challenging situations like occlusions and manipulations.
*   **Novel Architectural Contributions:** The introduction of entity-specific deformation strategies (HexPlane for humans and Cubic Hermite Splines for objects) tailored to their distinct kinematic properties is a novel contribution. The cross-attention-based HOI module, which learns bidirectional dependencies and applies interaction-aware corrections, is central to its improved performance in complex interaction scenarios.
*   **Foundation for Downstream Applications:** The high-fidelity and temporally consistent reconstruction capabilities of HOIGS have implications for a range of applications. These include:
    *   **Immersive Virtual Reality and Metaverse:** Enabling the creation of more realistic and interactive digital avatars and environments where precise human-object interactions are crucial.
    *   **3D Animation and Content Creation:** Streamlining the generation of complex animations with natural human-object interactions, reducing manual effort.
    *   **Human-Robot Interaction:** Providing more accurate representations of human manipulation and interaction, which could benefit robotics research in areas such as learning from demonstration or collaborative tasks.
    *   **Human Behavior Analysis:** Offering improved tools for analyzing and synthesizing human actions involving objects.
*   **Robustness to External Components:** The demonstrated robustness of HOIGS to variations in external segmentation and depth estimation priors suggests a degree of practical applicability, as it can leverage a range of existing off-the-shelf tools without significant performance degradation.

While the current work relies on pre-computed SMPL-X parameters and diffusion models for initialization, the authors acknowledge these as limitations and propose future work towards integrating physics-based priors for deformable objects and developing a fully end-to-end pipeline to reduce dependence on fixed preprocessing. These directions indicate a pathway toward even more robust and autonomous HOI reconstruction systems. Overall, HOIGS represents a step forward in robustly perceiving and rendering dynamic interactions between humans and objects from monocular video, addressing a long-standing challenge in computer graphics and vision.
overview:
## Understanding Human-Object Interaction in 4D

The reconstruction of dynamic scenes from monocular video is a significant challenge in computer vision, particularly when those scenes involve complex human-object interactions (HOI). Whether it is a person picking up a backpack, manipulating a tool, or interacting with furniture, capturing these moments in 3D over time (4D) requires modeling both the articulated motion of the human body and the rigid or semi-rigid motion of the objects involved. High-quality reconstruction of these interactions is essential for advancing technologies in virtual reality, 3D animation, and the development of immersive digital content.

![Human-object interaction reconstruction](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04016v1/intro.jpg)
*Figure 1: HOIGS enables high-quality 4D reconstruction of complex human-object interactions from a single monocular video.*

Existing methodologies for dynamic scene reconstruction generally take one of two paths. Human-centric methods utilize parametric models like SMPL to capture body poses but often treat objects as static background or fail to represent them altogether, resulting in visual artifacts like "ghosting" where objects appear blurred or translucent. Conversely, general 4D dynamic reconstruction methods, such as 4D Gaussian Splatting, treat all moving elements in a scene uniformly. While flexible, these general methods often struggle with the fine-grained details of human articulation and the specific rigid trajectories of manipulated objects, especially during close contact or occlusion.

HOIGS (Human-Object Interaction Gaussian Splatting) addresses these limitations by introducing a framework that explicitly disentangles human and object representations. By applying specialized motion models to each entity and coupling them through an interaction-aware refinement module, the system captures the bidirectional dependencies that define realistic interactions.

## Disentangled Representations for Humans and Objects

A core principle of HOIGS is the recognition that humans and objects possess fundamentally different physical and kinematic properties. The human body is articulated and capable of complex deformations, whereas manipulated objects often move as rigid bodies following continuous paths. To capture these nuances, HOIGS separates the scene into three distinct components: the human, the interacting object, and the static background.

![HOIGS Pipeline](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04016v1/x1.png)
*Figure 2: The HOIGS pipeline disentangles the scene into human, object, and background components, using entity-specific deformation models.*

The framework utilizes 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) as its primary rendering engine. In this representation, the scene is composed of millions of small, 3D ellipsoids (Gaussians), each defined by its position, rotation, scale, color, and opacity. For a dynamic scene, these Gaussians must move and deform over time. HOIGS assigns different "motion laws" to the Gaussians representing the human and those representing the object, allowing each to be optimized according to its specific nature.

## Articulated Human Modeling with HexPlanes and SMPL-X

For the human component, the framework adopts a hybrid approach that combines implicit spatial-temporal features with explicit parametric priors. The human body is represented in a canonical (T-pose) space. To handle the deformation from this canonical space to the dynamic posed space seen in each video frame, HOIGS employs a HexPlane-based feature representation.

The HexPlane decomposes a 4D space into six planes (XY, XT, YT, ZT, YZ, XZ) to efficiently encode spatial and temporal features. For any point in space at time $t$, features are sampled from these planes and processed by a shallow Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). This MLP predicts the necessary Gaussian attributes, such as position offsets, rotations, and skinning weights.

To ensure the reconstructed human remains anatomically plausible, the framework leverages SMPL-X, a sophisticated parametric human model. The final position of the human Gaussians in world space is determined via Linear Blend Skinning (LBS), driven by the SMPL-X pose parameters. This combination of feature-based learning and parametric priors allows the model to capture both the general articulation of the body and fine-grained surface details like clothing wrinkles or facial expressions.

## Rigid Object Trajectories via Cubic Hermite Splines

Interacting objects present a different challenge. Unlike the articulated human body, many manipulated objects are rigid but follow complex, non-linear trajectories as they are moved. To model this, the authors introduce a trajectory representation based on Cubic Hermite Splines (CHS).

Instead of predicting the object's position frame-by-frame—which can lead to jittery or discontinuous motion—the motion is modeled as a continuous curve. The object's path is defined by a set of learnable control points (positions) at specific keyframes and corresponding velocity vectors (tangents).

![Object trajectory modeling](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04016v1/x2.png)
*Figure 3: Object motion is parameterized using Cubic Hermite Splines, ensuring smooth and continuous trajectories in both position and velocity.*

For any intermediate time $t$ between keyframes $i$ and $i+1$, the position of the object is calculated using the interpolation formula:

$$P(t) = (2t^3 - 3t^2 + 1)P_i + (t^3 - 2t^2 + t)\tau_i + (-2t^3 + 3t^2)P_{i+1} + (t^3 - t^2)\tau_{i+1}$$

In this equation, $P_i$ represents the position at keyframe $i$, and $\tau_i$ represents the tangent (velocity) at that keyframe. This spline-based approach ensures that both the position and the velocity of the object remain continuous throughout the sequence. The object's rotation is handled separately using Spherical Linear Interpolation (SLERP) between keyframe rotations, while its scale is kept constant to enforce physical rigidity.

## Modeling Mutual Dependencies: The Interaction Module

The most distinct contribution of the HOIGS framework is the Interaction-aware (HOI) module. In real-world interactions, human motion and object motion are not independent; the hand's position is dictated by the object it holds, and the object's trajectory is a direct result of human manipulation.

To capture this mutual influence, the framework uses a cross-attention mechanism. First, features are extracted for both entities. For the human, the body is divided into 16 semantic parts (e.g., head, torso, left hand, right hand). Features for each part are averaged from the HexPlane to create human part tokens $F_{Human}$. For the object, continuous motion features $F_{Object}$ are generated from the spline velocities and learnable embeddings.

![Interaction Module](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04016v1/HOI_SUB_3.jpg)
*Figure 4: The bidirectional cross-attention module models how the human and object influence each other's motion during interaction.*

The module performs bidirectional cross-attention:
1.  **Human-to-Object:** The object "queries" the human features to adjust its own trajectory based on which body part it is near.
2.  **Object-to-Human:** The human parts "query" the object features to refine their pose based on the object's motion.

The queries $Q$, keys $K$, and values $V$ are projected from these features. A distance-based mask is applied to the attention calculation to ensure that only relevant body parts (like the hands) influence the object, while distant parts (like the feet during a hand-based task) are ignored. The output of this module is a set of residual corrections—$F'_{Human}$ and $F'_{Object}$—which are added to the baseline deformations to refine the SMPL-X pose and the object's position. This ensures that the hand and object stay perfectly aligned even during rapid movement or occlusion.

## Joint Optimization and Training

The entire system is trained by minimizing the difference between the rendered images and the original video frames. The optimization process jointly refines the human deformation field, the object splines, the interaction module parameters, and the static background.

The total objective function $L$ is a weighted combination of several specialized loss terms:

$$L = \lambda_{human} L_{human} + \lambda_{object} L_{object} + \lambda_{scene} L_{scene} + \lambda_{depth} L_{depth}$$

- **Photometric Losses ($L_{human}$, $L_{object}$, $L_{scene}$):** These measure the color and structural similarity between the rendered views and the ground truth frames for each specific region (human, object, or background).
- **Depth Loss ($L_{depth}$):** This ensures the 3D structure is consistent with depth estimates provided by off-the-shelf depth estimation models, helping to resolve ambiguities in the monocular view.
- **Regularization:** Additional terms are used to maintain the stability of the human body shape and facial expressions.

By optimizing these terms together, the model learns to distribute the "responsibility" for each pixel correctly between the human, the object, and the background, leading to a coherent 3D scene representation.

## Quantitative and Qualitative Evaluation

HOIGS was evaluated on several prominent datasets, including HOSNeRF, BEHAVE, and ARCTIC, which contain diverse sequences of people interacting with objects like backpacks, chairs, and tools.

Quantitatively, HOIGS consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines across multiple metrics. On the HOSNeRF dataset, it achieved a $PSNR$ (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) of 25.89, significantly higher than general 4DGS methods (24.96) and specialized human-centric models like ExAvatar (24.35). Lower $LPIPS$ scores—a metric that measures perceptual similarity—further confirmed that the reconstructions are visually more realistic and closer to the original video.

![Qualitative Results](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04016v1/ours_results.jpg)
*Figure 5: Comparative results demonstrate that HOIGS produces sharper interaction details and fewer artifacts compared to existing methods.*

Qualitatively, the benefit of the disentangled representation is evident. While other methods often produce "ghosting" artifacts where the human and object seem to merge or fade, HOIGS maintains clear boundaries and sharp textures. The interaction module is particularly effective in hand-object contact zones, where it prevents the object from "drifting" away from the hand—a common failure mode in previous 4D reconstruction frameworks.

Ablation studies performed by the authors confirmed that both the spline-based object modeling and the cross-attention interaction module are critical. Removing the interaction module, for example, led to a measurable drop in reconstruction quality, as the system could no longer precisely coordinate the dependent motions of the human and the object.

## Significance and Future Outlook

The introduction of HOIGS represents a shift in how dynamic human-object interactions are handled in the context of Gaussian Splatting. By explicitly modeling the "dialogue" between human and object motion through cross-attention, the framework achieves a level of fidelity that was previously difficult to reach with monocular video.

The system's ability to run inference at real-time speeds (over 40 frames per second) makes it a viable candidate for practical applications in content creation. Users can take a standard video and transform it into a high-quality 4D asset that can be viewed from any angle.

Despite its performance, the authors note that the method currently relies on pre-computed human pose parameters (SMPL-X) and masks from off-the-shelf tools. Future research could focus on making the system more end-to-end, potentially learning these priors directly during the optimization. Additionally, while the current spline model is ideal for rigid objects, expanding the framework to handle highly deformable objects (like cloth or soft bags) remains an area for exploration. Nevertheless, HOIGS provides a robust foundation for capturing the complexities of the physical world in a digital, interactive format.

keyInsights: Disentangling human and object representations and applying specialized deformation strategies for each entity (articulated human vs. rigid object) significantly improves the accuracy of complex HOI reconstruction.

Explicitly modeling bidirectional human-object dependencies through a cross-attention mechanism effectively mitigates interaction artifacts and ensures more physically plausible motion, even in close contact or occlusion.

Leveraging robust object initialization with diffusion priors and incorporating depth supervision enhances geometric fidelity, particularly for plausibly completing 3D geometry in occluded object regions.
originalProblem: Human-centric scene reconstruction methods often neglect dynamic objects, leading to artifacts or incomplete representations in human-object interaction scenarios.
General dynamic scene reconstruction (4D Gaussian Splatting) struggles to capture high-frequency details of human articulation and rigid object movements due to over-smoothing.
Accurately modeling the complex, interdependent spatial and temporal relationships between humans and objects from monocular video remains a challenge.
results: HOIGS achieved the highest PSNR and lowest LPIPS across HOSNeRF (PSNR 25.89, LPIPS 0.114), BEHAVE (PSNR 31.51, LPIPS 0.034), and ARCTIC datasets, outperforming 4DGS and human-centric baselines.

Produced photorealistic novel views without common artifacts such as ghosting, floating geometry, or body-background overlap, maintaining high visual fidelity in interaction regions under severe occlusion.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of the CHS-based object deformation (approx. 0.5 PSNR gain) and the explicit cross-attention HOI module (approx. 0.65 PSNR gain) to the overall performance.
solution: Employs entity-specific deformation baselines: a HexPlane-based model with SMPL-X priors for humans and Cubic Hermite Splines for object motion, tailored to their distinct kinematic properties.

Introduces a cross-attention-based Human-Object Interaction (HOI) module to explicitly model bidirectional dependencies between humans and objects, incorporating a distance mask to filter non-interacting elements.
Applies interaction-aware residual corrections to SMPL-X pose parameters and object Gaussian mean offsets, derived from the HOI module, to ensure interaction-consistent trajectories.
summary.summary: The HOIGS framework reconstructs dynamic human-object interactions from monocular video by disentangling human and object motions with entity-specific deformation models and unifying them via a cross-attention interaction module. This approach achieved high visual quality and geometric accuracy on HOSNeRF, BEHAVE, and ARCTIC datasets, effectively reducing common reconstruction artifacts.

title: HOIGS: Human-Object Interaction Gaussian Splatting
